topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rejection email sat glowing on the screen, a stark white insult against the muted backdrop of my cramped dorm room. I stared at the words—'Unfortunately, your application was not selected for the scholarship.'—and felt a cold, heavy pressure settle in my chest, squeezing the air from my lungs. I slowly closed the laptop, the click of the lid sounding deafeningly final in the silence. My hands clenched into fists on the worn fabric of my hoodie, the knuckles turning white. I didn't bother to look at my textbooks scattered across the desk; they seemed irrelevant now, just paper mocking my ambition. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space like a caged animal, the rhythm of my steps sharp and uneven. Every movement felt weighted, as if gravity had suddenly increased tenfold, dragging me down into a profound sense of unfairness.

[story 2]

I reread the rejection letter three times, each time the same hollow echo bouncing around in my head. The crisp edges of the paper seemed to mock the softness I had hoped for. A slow, tight smile stretched across my face, utterly devoid of warmth. I picked up my phone, intending to scroll through social media, but my fingers hovered over the screen, trembling slightly. I felt a sudden, intense heat rising in my neck, a flush of anger that burned beneath my skin. I tossed the phone onto the bedspread with unnecessary force, the sound sharp and dismissive. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent sprawl of the campus, feeling a profound sense of isolation. The world outside seemed vibrant and full of opportunities, while I felt encased in a thick, suffocating layer of disappointment.

[story 3]

"No," I whispered into the empty room, the sound raspy and weak. The official notification had landed like a physical blow, knocking the breath right out of me. I sank onto the edge of my desk chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my sudden weight. I ran both hands through my hair, pulling at the strands roughly, seeking some physical outlet for the churning turmoil inside. My jaw was tight, muscles visibly straining beneath the skin. I stood up, moving toward the door, not with resolve, but with a heavy, dragging gait. As I reached the hallway, I paused, turning back one last time to look at the neatly organized shelves—symbols of the future I was supposed to be building. A harsh, humorless laugh escaped my lips, a dry, brittle sound that held no joy whatsoever.
